one_line_takeaway: Federated PEFT is a soft target for jailbreaking: malicious clients tuning <1% of parameters can push a safety-aligned LLM to ~80% attack success, and existing robust-aggregation and post-hoc re-alignment defenses either fail under realistic data heterogeneity or restore safety only at a steep utility cost.
key_finding: Base models are well-aligned (ASR <4% at Round 0), but PaaA drives ASR up to ~80%+ with only a small malicious-client fraction and <1% trainable parameters. LoRA is both the most useful (consistent downstream gains) and the most vulnerable (ASR >70% by Round 20 in most cases), while (IA)3 and LayerNorm show moderate susceptibility (typically 40-60% ASR). Defenses are inadequate: classic RASs (Median, GeoMed) fail outright (ASR >30.7%); the stronger DnC and ClippedClustering suppress the attack under IID single-domain MedQA data (ASR <=2%) but collapse under heterogeneous non-IID client data (ASR >80%). PPSA can pull ASR back below 10% within a few rounds, but incurs an 'alignment tax' that degrades target-task utility (e.g., LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct loses up to ~20% accuracy on the non-target MetaMathQA task and ~3% on MedQA). Both the attack and re-alignment take effect in fewer than 5 communication rounds.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'PEFT-as-an-Attack' (PaaA), a jailbreak threat specific to Federated Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (FedPEFT). Its own contribution is (1) formulating and empirically demonstrating that malicious clients can exploit the PEFT modules exchanged during federated fine-tuning to strip the safety alignment of a frozen safety-aligned PLM, generating harmful content, even when they touch <1% of parameters and cannot modify the frozen base weights; (2) a comparative vulnerability study across three PEFT methods (LoRA, (IA)3, LayerNorm) and four PLMs; and (3) an evaluation of two families of existing defenses (Robust Aggregation Schemes and Post-PEFT Safety Alignment), showing both are inadequate. The authors also integrate their attack into the Blades FL-security benchmark for reproducibility. Note: the defenses (RASs like DnC/ClippedClustering, and PPSA) are NOT the paper's invention; they are prior work the paper repurposes and stress-tests against PaaA.
threat_model: Adversaries control a minority subset of FedPEFT clients (0-5 of 15 in experiments; honest clients assumed to be the majority >50%). Compromised clients follow the standard FedPEFT protocol (same communication pattern, same ClientOpt local training) but train their local PEFT module on injected toxic QA data (sampled from BeaverTails 'unsafe' category). They cannot alter the frozen base-model weights w; they only influence the shared PEFT parameters theta via FedAvg aggregation. Attack objective: maximize likelihood of harmful outputs on malicious instructions while staying stealthy (benign-task accuracy on validation stays intact, evading standard metric-based detection). The central server is assumed honest and may deploy defenses; the PLM weights are assumed to remain securely frozen across all clients.
limitations: No new effective defense is proposed; the paper only shows existing ones fail, leaving the core threat unmitigated (authors defer robust PPSA/in-training alignment to future work). The RAS non-IID stress test is run on only one configuration (Phi-3.5-Mini-Instruct + LoRA, 3 malicious of 15), limiting generality of the 'RASs collapse under heterogeneity' claim. The threat model is favorable to defenders (honest server, honest majority >50%, provably frozen base weights); a compromised server or majority attacker is out of scope. Models are deliberately not trained to convergence on full datasets (subsets over limited rounds), so absolute utility numbers are indicative rather than best-case. ASR relies on an OpenAI-API harmfulness judge that the authors themselves flag as approximate and imperfect. Defenses were not designed for this threat model, so their failure is partly expected.

datasets_used: MedQA (medical multiple-choice QA, downstream/target utility task)
MetaMathQA (math QA augmented from GSM8K and MATH, downstream/target utility task)
BeaverTails (unsafe category, source of malicious/toxic fine-tuning data for the attack)
CAI-Conversation-Harmless (AI-generated constitutional-AI alignment data used for PPSA defense)
quantitative_results: Trainable-parameter ratio across PEFT methods/models: 0.001% to 0.78% (Table I; e.g. LLaMA-2-7B-Chat LoRA 40.0M params = 0.59%, LayerNorm 0.3M = 0.004%; Phi-3.5 LoRA 29.9M = 0.78%)
Base-model ASR before attack: <4% (Round 0); Table II base Phi-3.5 AdvBench/JailbreakBench ASR = 1.3% / 1.3%
PaaA peak ASR up to ~80%+; LoRA >70% ASR by Round 20 in most cases; (IA)3 and LayerNorm typically 40-60%
RAS defense (Phi-3.5 + LoRA, 3 malicious of 15), MedQA-only IID: ClippedClustering AdvBench ASR 0.0%, JailbreakBench 1.9%; DnC AdvBench 1.0%, JailbreakBench 1.2%; but Mean/Median/GeoMed ASR ~77-86%
RAS defense, mixed-domain non-IID (6 MedQA + 6 MetaMathQA): ALL RASs fail, e.g. ClippedClustering AdvBench 81.0% / JailbreakBench 88.3%, DnC 82.5% / 87.7%
Utility under RAS (Table II): base MedQA accuracy 56.9%, MetaMathQA 80.0%; MedQA-only training raises MedQA to ~64.7% but lowers MetaMathQA to ~71.1%
PPSA (LoRA on MedQA, 14 rounds): ASR spikes to 57% (AdvBench) and 65% (JailbreakBench) within first 5 rounds, then alignment clients (rounds 11-14) cut ASR to below 10%
Alignment tax: LLaMA-3.2-3B-Instruct accuracy drops up to ~3% on MedQA and up to ~20% on MetaMathQA after PPSA
LoRA benign-condition MedQA accuracy trajectory spans roughly 28%-67% across the four models over 25 rounds
baselines_compared: PEFT methods compared against each other: LoRA vs (IA)3 vs LayerNorm (attack susceptibility and utility)
Robust Aggregation Schemes: Median, GeoMed, DnC, ClippedClustering, all vs the Mean (FedAvg) baseline
Post-PEFT Safety Alignment (PPSA) as an alternative defense direction
Prior federated-jailbreak study [40] (limited to single LoRA + LLaMA-2) contrasted as narrower scope
